data.title: TriLinc Global Impact Fund reports $283M portfolio, $1.21B funded to 103 borrowers
data.description:
TriLinc Global Impact Fund reported $283 million in portfolio assets and $1.21 billion funded to 103 borrowers.

Key Highlights:

  • Portfolio assets of approximately $283 million as of March 31, 2026, including fair-valued investments in borrowers.
  • Weighted average loan size ~ $10 million and weighted average duration 0.4 years, indicating short-term portfolio tenor.
  • TGIF has funded about $1.210 billion in aggregate investments to 103 borrower companies globally, including $104.7 million in temporary investments.
  • Approximately $904.6 million (75% of total invested) has been received in full aggregate transaction repayments to date.

Original SEC Filing: TriLinc Global Impact Fund LLC [ TRLC ] - 8-K - Apr. 23, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: High-End Cardholders Slow Their Airline Spending — WSJ
data.description:
By Ben Glickman

More American Express cardholders want refunds for their plane tickets.

Chief Financial Officer Christopher Le Caillec says on a call with analysts that airline spending on the company's cards grew in the first quarter, but that consumers and businesses slowed their spend in the latter weeks of March and in April due to the conflict in the Middle East. Le Caillec says the company also saw a spike in customer refund requests.

Multiple airlines in recent days have reported billions in additional fuel costs due to the conflict in the Mideast, leading to higher prices and trimmed schedules. Executives generally expect higher ticket prices to eat into demand.

American Express said that broader spending on its cards rose the most in three years, as profit and revenue topped Wall Street's estimates. Shares are falling more than 3% in midday trading.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Data Center Boom Is a Boon to Texas Instruments — Market Talk
data.description: In recent years, soaring demand for artificial intelligence and cloud computing has caused a massive expansion of data centers across the U.S., boosting the semiconductor business of Texas Instruments. Growth in its data center business has accelerated in the past three quarters, according to UBS analysts. The company's data center revenue surged about 90% year-over-year, analysts say, with the segment expected to continue to grow in the coming quarters. In particular, the company expects its application-specific engagements for AI to ramp up in the second half of 2026, according to Susquehanna analysts.(chris.kuo@wsj.com)

data.title: Corn firms on export demand, wheat up on US weather worries
data.description:
Key points:
  • USDA reports strong corn export sales, wheat gains on US Plains drought concerns
  • Soybeans dip on profit-taking and technical selling after recent highs
  • Higher oil prices and Hormuz blockage support grains, but funds' long positions add risk

By Karl Plume

U.S. corn futures firmed on Thursday on strong weekly export sales and as wheat futures climbed on concerns about drought-reduced production in the U.S. Plains.

Soybeans eased on technical selling and profit-taking after touching multiweek highs in the previous session, as traders assessed spring planting progress and weighed demand prospects amid ample global supplies.

Grains overall remained underpinned by higher oil prices as the Strait of Hormuz remained blocked. But managed funds hold sizeable net-long positions in corn and soybeans, which has left the markets vulnerable to profit-taking corrections.

Traders continued to monitor U.S. weather as some Midwest farmers raced to plant corn and soybeans ahead of rains expected in the coming days, while showers were seen offering relief to only portions of the drought-hit Plains wheat crop.

Chicago Board of Trade July corn (CN26) was up 3/4 cent at $4.63-1/2 a bushel at 11:08 a.m. CDT (1608 GMT), while CBOT July wheat (WN26) gained 8-3/4 cents to $6.15-3/4 a bushel. July soybeans (SN26) fell 5-1/4 cents to $11.74-1/4 a bushel after dropping below technical chart support at its 50-day moving average.

"Corn export sales were at the high end of expectations today, and that's helping support the corn. Also supporting the corn is the rally in wheat, which seems completely weather-related," said Jack Scoville, vice president at Price Futures Group. "But the beans don't have that love."

The U.S. Department of Agriculture said net old-crop U.S. corn export sales last week totalled 1.3 million metric tons, in line with trade estimates, while new-crop sales topped expectations at a marketing-year high of 440,100 tons. Net soybean sales of 364,600 tons were near the low end of trade expectations.

data.title: Hightower to Buy Bahnsen Group, a $9.5 Billion Wealth Management Practice — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Andrew Welsch

Hightower and The Bahnsen Group have long been close partners. Now they are embracing one another fully, with a deal for Hightower to acquire Bahnsen.

The Bahnsen Group, a wealth management practice that manages $9.5 billion in client assets, got its start in 2015, when founder David Bahnsen left Morgan Stanley. Hightower helped him make that exit, and The Bahnsen Group has been a Hightower partner for more than a decade, relying on its broker-dealer and registered investment advisor to conduct its business.

Hightower didn't disclose terms of the acquisition, which it expects to close in the third quarter. Hightower says the deal will allow it to invest more in The Bahnsen Group's brand and capabilities, and that the practice will be another "hub" within Hightower and a destination for like-minded advisors.

The Bahnsen Group will continue to operate under its own brand, but will add "A Hightower Company" to its name and logo.

"This transaction reflects our focus on building enduring, scaled advisory businesses," Hightower CEO Larry Restieri said in a statement. "The Bahnsen Group represents the type of franchise we are committed to further investing in for the long term."

Bahnsen said the deepening relationship with Hightower will be critical to his practice's next phase of growth. "With deeper integration across Hightower, we are well positioned to continue investing in our team and pursuing both organic growth and strategic opportunities," he said. "This venture leverages the strengths of each party to the benefit of our clients."

Bahnsen is ranked No. 83 on Barron's Top 100 Financial Advisors for 2025 and No. 22 on Barron's Top 100 Independent Advisors for 2025.

The Bahnsen Group has a team of approximately 100 staff members and operates 12 offices, including Newport Beach, Calif.; New York; Nashville, Tenn.; and Palm Beach, Fla.

Chicago-based Hightower got its start by helping breakaway advisors establish independent practices. It later began acquiring registered investment advisory firms. And last year, Hightower launched a strategy to centralize services and began offering advisors the option to operate under the Hightower brand.

In March, Hightower agreed to acquire Journey Strategic Wealth, a financial advice business with approximately $5 billion in assets under management.

Write to Andrew Welsch at andrew.welsch@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Vinci Sticks to 2026 Estimates After First-Quarter Revenue Decline
data.description:
By Cristina Gallardo

Vinci confirmed its forecasts for the year after a slight decrease in first-quarter revenue and despite uncertainty over the potential impact of the war in Iran.

The French construction and infrastructure company on Thursday said revenue for the period fell 0.3% on-year to 16.28 billion euros ($19.05 billion) on a reported basis. On a like-for-like basis, revenue was down 0.5%.

The result fell short of analysts' expectations of 16.61 billion euros, according to consensus estimates provided by Visible Alpha.

Vinci's order intake climbed 5% to 17.4 billion euros, while its order book reached a new record of 74.9 billion euros at the end of March.

In France, revenue fell 2% to 6.97 billion euros, while outside of France revenue increased 1.1% to 9.31 billion euros.

Vinci said the results marked a solid start to the year and stuck to its 2026 guidance, noting that the potential impact from the Iran war can't be assessed yet.

It said it had confidence in growing demand for electrification and digitalization infrastructure, driven by a push for more sovereignty in several regions.

Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com

data.title: Adyen to buy software firm Talon.One for $878 million
data.description:
Payment services firm Adyen on Thursday agreed to buy German software company Talon.One for a total cash consideration of 750 million euros ($878 million).

Following the transaction, Talon.One co-founders Christoph Gerber and Sebastian Haas will reinvest a "meaningful portion" of their proceeds in newly issued shares in the Amsterdam-listed firm, Adyen said in a statement.

  • The company also reported a 20% rise in first-quarter revenue, on a constant currency basis, to nearly 621 million euros.

  • It confirmed its financial objectives for 2026.

  • The acquisition is expected to close in the second half of the year, pending conditions and regulatory approvals.

($1 = 0.8543 euros)
